claims.claim: CSS grid masonry layout can be achieved natively using `grid-template-rows: masonry`, but as of January 2021 this was experimental and only available in Firefox behind a feature flag.
claims.quote: Masonry layout using `grid-template-rows: masonry`... the masonry specification... available only in Firefox behind a feature flag

claims.claim: The CSS `align-tracks: stretch` property was proposed as part of the masonry spec to fix uneven bottom edges in grid masonry layouts, but it had known issues when items span multiple columns.
claims.quote: The masonry specification also allows for a new property: `align-tracks`, which you can set to `stretch`

claims.claim: CSS columns (`column-width`) provide a viable fallback for masonry layouts, but item order flows down columns (top-to-bottom per column) rather than across rows, which differs from CSS grid ordering behavior.
claims.quote: The order of items goes down the columns as opposed to across the rows with css grid. Columns: ⬇️ Grid: ➡️

claims.claim: Setting `column-width` (rather than `column-count`) creates responsive multi-column layouts without media queries, because it specifies a minimum column width before columns collapse.
claims.quote: Setting a `column-width` will set a minimum width of a column before columns need to shrink down

claims.claim: Progressive enhancement for CSS masonry can be implemented using the `@supports` rule for automatic browser-based feature detection, applying masonry grid to supporting browsers and CSS columns as the fallback without changing HTML markup.
claims.quote: @supports CSS rule — Enables automatic browser-based feature detection and fallback application without HTML changes
